title: Install LLVM toolchain and Python dependencies
description: Set up the experimental environment with LLVM 17+ for IR-level optimization and Python libraries for Shapley computation, statistical analysis, and visualization.

steps.step1: Check if LLVM 17+ is available (llvm-17, opt-17, clang-17). If not, install via apt: sudo apt-get install -y llvm-17 clang-17. Fall back to whatever LLVM version is available (>=14) if 17 is not in repos.
steps.step2: Install Python dependencies: pip install numpy scipy matplotlib seaborn pandas scikit-learn shapiq networkx tqdm joblib
steps.step3: Verify the toolchain: run 'opt --version' (or opt-17 --version), 'clang --version', 'python3 -c "import shapiq; import numpy; import scipy"'
steps.step4: Create the project directory structure: mkdir -p data/polybench data/cbench results/shapley_values results/interactions results/selection results/figures
steps.step5: Document the exact LLVM version and Python package versions in a file results/environment.txt for reproducibility

title: Download and compile benchmark programs to LLVM IR
description: Obtain PolyBench/C 4.2 and a subset of cBench benchmarks, compile each to unoptimized LLVM IR (.ll files) which serve as input to the opt tool for all experiments.

steps.step1: Download PolyBench/C 4.2 from https://sourceforge.net/projects/polybench/ or the GitHub mirror. Extract to data/polybench/. PolyBench contains 30 numerical kernels across 6 categories (linear-algebra, datamining, stencils, medley).
steps.step2: For each PolyBench kernel, compile to unoptimized LLVM IR: clang -O0 -emit-llvm -S -DPOLYBENCH_TIME -DMINI_DATASET -I utilities/ utilities/polybench.c <kernel>.c -o data/polybench/<name>.ll. Use MINI_DATASET to keep compilation fast.
steps.step3: For cBench diversity: write 10 small representative C programs covering different domains (string processing, sorting, bitwise operations, math, linked list, matrix multiply, recursive algorithms, hash table, binary search, simple interpreter). Each program should be 50-200 lines. Compile each to unoptimized LLVM IR: clang -O0 -emit-llvm -S <program>.c -o data/cbench/<name>.ll
steps.step4: Alternatively, if cBench is available via CompilerGym or direct download, use 10 programs from it. Prioritize programs that compile quickly (<100ms for opt -O3).
steps.step5: Validate all .ll files: run 'opt -O0 <file>.ll -o /dev/null' to confirm they are valid IR. Record the baseline (unoptimized) IR instruction count for each program using: opt -stats -disable-output <file>.ll 2>&1 | grep 'Number of instructions'. Alternatively, count instructions with: grep -c '^  ' <file>.ll or use llvm-as + llvm-dis pipeline.
steps.step6: Create a Python utility function count_ir_instructions(ll_file) that parses LLVM IR and counts instructions. Validate against opt -stats output.
steps.step7: Save a manifest file data/benchmark_manifest.json listing all programs with paths, categories, and baseline instruction counts. Target: 30-40 programs total.

title: Identify and catalog LLVM optimization passes
description: Determine the set of LLVM transform passes to study. Focus on the most impactful passes from the -O2/-O3 pipelines, excluding analysis-only and trivially low-impact passes.

steps.step1: Extract the pass list from LLVM's -O3 pipeline: opt -O3 -debug-pass-manager -disable-output /dev/null 2>&1 (for new pass manager) or opt -O3 -debug-pass=Structure -disable-output /dev/null 2>&1 (for legacy pass manager). Parse the output to get ordered pass names.
steps.step2: Alternatively, use a curated list of the ~30 most common LLVM transform passes. This curated list should include: -adce (aggressive dead code elimination), -bdce (bit-tracking dead code elimination), -constmerge (merge duplicate globals), -dce (dead code elimination), -dse (dead store elimination), -early-cse (early common subexpression elimination), -globaldce (dead global elimination), -globalopt (global variable optimizer), -gvn (global value numbering), -indvars (induction variable simplification), -inline (function inlining), -instcombine (combine redundant instructions), -jump-threading (jump threading), -lcssa (loop-closed SSA form), -licm (loop invariant code motion), -loop-deletion (delete dead loops), -loop-idiom (recognize loop idioms), -loop-reduce (loop strength reduction), -loop-rotate (rotate loops), -loop-simplifycfg (simplify loop CFG), -loop-unroll (unroll loops), -loop-unswitch (unswitch loops), -mem2reg (promote memory to register), -memcpyopt (memcpy optimization), -reassociate (reassociate expressions), -sccp (sparse conditional constant propagation), -simplifycfg (simplify control flow graph), -sroa (scalar replacement of aggregates), -tailcallelim (tail call elimination), -correlated-propagation (correlated value propagation).
steps.step3: Verify each pass works with the current LLVM version: for each pass, run opt <pass_flag> <test_file>.ll -o /dev/null and check for errors. Remove any passes that error out. Adapt pass names to new pass manager syntax if needed (e.g., --passes='instcombine' instead of -instcombine).
steps.step4: Determine the canonical ordering: extract the order in which these passes appear in the -O3 pipeline. This ordering is used when applying a subset of passes (maintaining relative order from -O3).
steps.step5: Save the final pass list with canonical ordering to data/pass_catalog.json: [{"name": "instcombine", "flag": "--passes=instcombine", "order": 1, "category": "peephole"}, ...]
steps.step6: Target: n=30 passes. This gives a feasible computation space while covering all major optimization categories (peephole, loop, memory, control flow, interprocedural).

title: Build the compilation oracle function
description: Create a Python function that takes a program and a subset of passes, applies them in canonical order using opt, and returns the resulting IR instruction count. This is the characteristic function v(S) of the cooperative game.

steps.step1: Write a Python module src/oracle.py with function compile_and_measure(ll_file, pass_subset, pass_catalog) that: (a) orders the passes in pass_subset according to canonical ordering, (b) constructs the opt command: opt --passes='pass1,pass2,...' input.ll -o /tmp/output.ll, (c) runs the command via subprocess with a 30-second timeout, (d) counts IR instructions in the output, (e) returns the instruction count.
steps.step2: Implement caching: use a dictionary keyed by (program_name, frozenset(pass_subset)) to avoid recomputing identical coalitions. Persist cache to disk as a JSON file for resumability.
steps.step3: Implement the characteristic function v(S) = (baseline_count - optimized_count) / baseline_count, giving the fractional code size reduction. v(empty set) = 0 by definition.
steps.step4: Test the oracle on 3 programs with: empty set (should return 0), full set (should approximate -O3 reduction), single passes (sanity check that each pass alone has a non-negative or plausible effect).
steps.step5: Benchmark the oracle speed: time 100 random coalition evaluations. Record median and p95 latency. Target: <100ms per evaluation for PolyBench kernels.
steps.step6: Add a batch evaluation function that evaluates multiple coalitions in parallel using joblib.Parallel with n_jobs=2 (2 CPU cores).

title: Experiment 1: Shapley Value Attribution via Monte Carlo sampling
description: Compute approximate Shapley values for all ~30 passes on each benchmark program using Monte Carlo permutation sampling. This quantifies each pass's individual contribution to optimization, accounting for all possible orderings and interactions.

steps.step1: Implement Monte Carlo Shapley value estimation in src/shapley.py: function compute_shapley_mc(program, passes, num_permutations, seed). For each permutation: (a) randomly permute passes, (b) iterate through the permutation computing marginal contributions v(S+{i}) - v(S), (c) accumulate marginal contributions per pass. Return mean and std of Shapley values.
steps.step2: Run with M=500 permutations per program across all ~35 benchmark programs. Use 3 different random seeds (42, 123, 456) for the Monte Carlo sampling to assess estimation variance. Each seed produces an independent Shapley value estimate.
steps.step3: Parallelize across programs using joblib with n_jobs=2. Each program requires 500 permutations x 30 passes = 15,000 coalition evaluations. At ~50ms each = ~12.5 minutes per program. Total for 35 programs: ~7.3 hours sequential, ~3.7 hours with 2 cores.
steps.step4: Save raw results to results/shapley_values/shapley_raw_{seed}.json: for each program, the Shapley value vector (mean and std across permutations) for all 30 passes.
steps.step5: Compute convergence diagnostics: for each program, track the running mean of Shapley values as a function of permutation count (at M=50, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500). Save to results/shapley_values/convergence.json.
steps.step6: Aggregate results across seeds: compute the mean and standard deviation of Shapley values across the 3 seeds. This gives both within-seed variance (estimation noise) and across-seed variance (stability of estimates).
steps.step7: Runtime estimate: ~3.7 hours with 2 cores. This is the most computationally intensive step.

title: Baseline 1: Leave-One-Out (LOO) ablation attribution
description: Compute leave-one-out attributions as the simple baseline: for each pass, measure the performance drop when removing it from the full set. This ignores interaction effects and serves as a comparison to show why Shapley values are needed.

steps.step1: For each benchmark program: (a) compute v(N) = value of the full pass set, (b) for each pass i, compute v(N \ {i}), (c) LOO attribution for pass i = v(N) - v(N \ {i}).
steps.step2: This requires only n+1 = 31 evaluations per program (fast: ~1.5 seconds per program).
steps.step3: Run for all 35 programs. Save results to results/shapley_values/loo_attribution.json.
steps.step4: Compare LOO rankings with Shapley value rankings: compute Spearman rank correlation between LOO attribution and Shapley values for each program. Report mean and std across programs.
steps.step5: Identify passes where LOO and Shapley disagree most — these are passes involved in strong interactions.

title: Baseline 2: Random sampling baseline for pass selection
description: Evaluate random pass selection as a baseline for the pass selection experiments. Sample random subsets of k passes and measure their optimization performance, establishing the performance distribution that any intelligent selection method must beat.

steps.step1: For each program, for each k in {5, 10, 15, 20, 25}: sample 100 random subsets of size k from the 30 passes. Apply each subset (in canonical order) and record the code size reduction.
steps.step2: Use 3 random seeds (42, 123, 456) for the sampling to assess variance.
steps.step3: For each (program, k), record: mean, std, min, max, and median code size reduction across the 100 random subsets.
steps.step5: Also compute the -O2 and -O3 equivalent baselines: run opt -O2 and opt -O3 on each program and record the code size reduction. Save to results/selection/standard_levels.json.
steps.step6: Runtime estimate: 35 programs x 5 k-values x 100 samples = 17,500 evaluations. At ~50ms each = ~15 minutes total.

title: Experiment 2: Pairwise Shapley Interaction Indices
description: Compute pairwise Shapley Interaction Indices (SII) for the top-20 passes (by average Shapley value) to quantify synergies and antagonisms between pass pairs. This is the core novel contribution.

steps.step1: Select the top-20 passes by average Shapley value across all programs (from Experiment 1 results).
steps.step2: Implement pairwise SII computation in src/interactions.py. For each pair (i,j), use Monte Carlo estimation: sample random coalitions S from N\{i,j}, compute the interaction delta = v(S+{i,j}) - v(S+{i}) - v(S+{j}) + v(S), and average over samples weighted by the Shapley kernel.
steps.step3: Use the shapiq library if it supports custom game functions: shapiq.InteractionValues with the compilation oracle as the game function. Otherwise implement the Monte Carlo SII estimator from scratch using 200 coalition samples per pair.
steps.step4: Compute all C(20,2) = 190 pairwise interactions for each program. With 200 samples per pair, each requiring 4 coalition evaluations = 190 x 200 x 4 = 152,000 evaluations per program. At 50ms each = ~2.1 hours per program — TOO SLOW.
steps.step5: EFFICIENCY OPTIMIZATION: Reuse coalition evaluations from Experiment 1. The 500 permutations already evaluate 15,000 coalitions. Extract pairwise interaction estimates directly from the permutation-based Shapley computation: for each permutation, track which pairs co-occur and estimate marginal interaction contributions. This avoids additional compilation runs.
steps.step6: Alternative efficient approach: Use a subset of 10-15 representative programs (5 PolyBench categories + 5 diverse small programs). Compute interactions only for these, reducing total computation.
steps.step7: For each program, produce a 20x20 interaction matrix. Save to results/interactions/interaction_matrix_{program}.json.
steps.step8: Compute statistical significance: for each interaction index, compute a 95% confidence interval from the Monte Carlo samples. Flag interactions where the CI excludes zero as statistically significant.
steps.step9: Runtime estimate: ~1-2 hours (reusing Experiment 1 data, subset of programs).

title: Experiment 3: ShapleyPass-Select greedy pass selection
description: Implement and evaluate the interaction-guided greedy pass selector. Compare against baselines (random, LOO top-k, Shapley top-k, -O2, -O3) to demonstrate that interaction information improves pass selection.

steps.step1: Implement ShapleyPass-Select in src/selector.py: greedy forward selection where score(p_i) = phi_i + lambda * sum_{p_j in S} I_{ij}. Start with S=empty, iteratively add the highest-scoring pass, stop when no pass improves performance or budget k is reached.
steps.step2: Implement baseline selectors: (a) Top-k-Shapley: select k passes with highest Shapley values (no interaction term, equivalent to lambda=0), (b) Top-k-LOO: select k passes with highest LOO attribution, (c) Random-k: random selection (from Baseline 2), (d) Greedy-ablation: start from full set, greedily remove the pass whose removal hurts least.
steps.step3: Run ShapleyPass-Select with lambda values in {0.0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0} to study the effect of the interaction weight. For each lambda, run on all programs and record the selected pass sets and their code size reduction at each k from 1 to 30.
steps.step4: Use 3 random seeds for any stochastic components (tie-breaking). For deterministic methods, seeds don't matter but record them for consistency.
steps.step5: For each method and each program, record: the sequence of selected passes, and the code size reduction at each step k=1..30.
steps.step6: Save results to results/selection/shapley_select.json, results/selection/topk_shapley.json, results/selection/topk_loo.json, results/selection/greedy_ablation.json.
steps.step7: Key comparison: at what k does each method reach 95% and 98% of -O3 code size reduction? Report this 'pass efficiency' metric.
steps.step8: Runtime estimate: ~30 minutes (most coalitions already cached from previous experiments).

title: Ablation 1: Effect of interaction term (lambda) on selection quality
description: Ablation study isolating the contribution of the interaction term in ShapleyPass-Select. Compare lambda=0 (pure Shapley, no interactions) vs lambda>0 (with interactions) to demonstrate that interaction information provides value beyond individual attributions.

steps.step1: From Experiment 3 results, extract performance curves for ShapleyPass-Select at lambda=0.0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0.
steps.step2: For each program, compute the area under the code-size-reduction curve (AUCR) from k=1 to k=30 for each lambda value. Higher AUCR means better pass selection efficiency.
steps.step3: Compute paired statistical tests: for each lambda>0 vs lambda=0, run a paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test across programs on AUCR. Report p-values.
steps.step4: Identify programs where the interaction term helps most (largest AUCR gap between lambda=0 and best lambda>0). Analyze what makes these programs special.

title: Ablation 2: Monte Carlo convergence and sample efficiency
description: Analyze how many Monte Carlo permutations are needed for stable Shapley value estimates. This validates our choice of M=500 and demonstrates the practical computational cost.

steps.step1: Using the convergence data from Experiment 1 (step 5), plot the running mean of Shapley values for the top-10 passes as a function of M (number of permutations) for 5 representative programs.
steps.step2: Compute the Spearman rank correlation between the pass ranking at M permutations and the final ranking at M=500, for M in {25, 50, 100, 150, 200, 300, 400}.
steps.step3: Determine the minimum M at which the top-10 ranking stabilizes (Spearman > 0.95 with the M=500 ranking) for 90% of programs.
steps.step4: Compare variance reduction: standard Monte Carlo vs stratified sampling (if implemented). If not, just report standard MC convergence.

title: Experiment 4: Cross-program transferability of interaction patterns
description: Analyze whether pass interaction patterns are consistent across programs, especially within program categories. This tests whether pre-computed interaction landscapes can transfer to new programs.

steps.step1: For the 10-15 programs with computed interaction matrices, compute pairwise Spearman correlation between their (flattened) interaction matrices.
steps.step2: Cluster programs by their interaction fingerprints using hierarchical clustering (Ward's method) on the correlation distance matrix.
steps.step3: Compare interaction-based clusters with program category labels (PolyBench categories: linear-algebra/blas, linear-algebra/kernels, linear-algebra/solvers, datamining, stencils, medley, and custom small programs). Compute Adjusted Rand Index.
steps.step4: Test transfer: for each program, use the average interaction matrix of the other programs in its cluster to guide ShapleyPass-Select. Compare performance to using the program's own interaction matrix.
steps.step5: Report: (a) average within-cluster Spearman correlation (target > 0.3), (b) average between-cluster correlation, (c) transfer selection performance gap.

title: Experiment 5: Computational cost analysis
description: Report the wall-clock time and compilation count for each experimental stage. Compare the cost of Shapley-based analysis with iterative compilation baselines.

steps.step1: Record wall-clock time for each major computation: (a) Shapley values (Experiment 1), (b) interaction indices (Experiment 2), (c) selection algorithms (Experiment 3).
steps.step2: Count total number of compilation (opt) invocations for each stage. Report cache hit rate.
steps.step3: Compare: to reach the same code size reduction as ShapleyPass-Select, how many random compilations would be needed? Plot the 'compilations to achieve X% of -O3' for random search vs ShapleyPass-Select.
steps.step4: Report amortization: once Shapley values/interactions are computed for a program, how fast is the selection step? (Should be near-instant since it's just arithmetic.)

title: Statistical evaluation of all success criteria
description: Systematically evaluate each success criterion defined in the proposal. This produces the key numerical claims for the paper.

steps.step1: Success Criterion 1: 'At least 30% of pass pairs show statistically significant non-zero Shapley interaction indices (p < 0.05 after Bonferroni correction)'. Count significant interactions per program and report the percentage. Average across programs.
steps.step2: Success Criterion 2: 'ShapleyPass-Select matches -O3 code size within 2% using at most 50% of passes (15 out of 30)'. For each program, find the minimum k at which ShapleyPass-Select achieves >= 98% of -O3 reduction. Report mean, median, and percentage of programs meeting the criterion.
steps.step3: Success Criterion 3: 'Cross-program interaction consistency: average Spearman correlation > 0.3 within program clusters'. Report from Experiment 4.
steps.step4: Success Criterion 4: 'ShapleyPass-Select outperforms random search and top-k Shapley baselines given equal compilation budget'. At budget = 15 passes, compare mean code size reduction of ShapleyPass-Select vs random-15 and top-15-Shapley. Run paired Wilcoxon tests.
steps.step5: Compile all results into results/success_criteria_evaluation.json with clear pass/fail for each criterion.
steps.step6: Also check refutation conditions: (a) are interactions negligible? (mean absolute interaction index near zero), (b) does Shapley-guided selection perform no better than LOO top-k?

title: Generate all paper figures and tables
description: Create publication-quality figures and tables for the research paper. Save as PDF and PNG.

steps.step1: Figure 1 — Shapley Value Bar Chart: horizontal bar chart of average Shapley values for all 30 passes, sorted by value. Error bars show std across programs. Color-code by pass category (loop, memory, peephole, control flow, interprocedural). Save as results/figures/fig1_shapley_values.pdf.
steps.step2: Figure 2 — LOO vs Shapley Scatter Plot: scatter plot comparing LOO attribution (x-axis) vs Shapley value (y-axis) for each pass, averaged across programs. Annotate the most divergent passes. Include Spearman correlation. Save as results/figures/fig2_loo_vs_shapley.pdf.
steps.step3: Figure 3 — Interaction Heatmap: 20x20 heatmap of pairwise Shapley interaction indices, averaged across representative programs. Annotate the top 5 synergistic and top 5 antagonistic pairs. Use hierarchical clustering to order passes. Save as results/figures/fig3_interaction_heatmap.pdf.
steps.step4: Figure 4 — Pass Selection Curves: line plot of code size reduction (y-axis) vs number of passes selected (x-axis, 1 to 30) for all methods: ShapleyPass-Select (best lambda), Top-k-Shapley, Top-k-LOO, Random (with error band), Greedy-ablation. Horizontal lines for -O2 and -O3. Average across programs with shaded std bands. Save as results/figures/fig4_selection_curves.pdf.
steps.step5: Figure 5 — Convergence Plot: Shapley value estimate convergence as a function of permutation count M. Show 5 representative passes on one representative program, with 95% CI bands. Save as results/figures/fig5_convergence.pdf.
steps.step6: Figure 6 — Transferability Dendrogram: hierarchical clustering dendrogram of programs based on interaction matrix similarity. Color leaves by program category. Save as results/figures/fig6_transferability.pdf.
steps.step7: Figure 7 — Lambda Ablation: box plot or bar chart of AUCR (area under code-reduction curve) for lambda=0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0. Show significance markers. Save as results/figures/fig7_lambda_ablation.pdf.
steps.step8: Table 1 — Top-10 passes by Shapley value with their LOO ranking, average value, and std. Save as results/figures/table1_top_passes.csv.
steps.step9: Table 2 — Pass selection comparison: for each method, report mean code size reduction at k=10 and k=15, percentage of -O3 achieved, and number of programs where 98% of -O3 is reached. Save as results/figures/table2_selection_comparison.csv.
steps.step10: Table 3 — Top-5 synergistic and top-5 antagonistic pass pairs with their interaction indices and brief explanation. Save as results/figures/table3_interactions.csv.
steps.step11: Use consistent styling: matplotlib with seaborn style, font size 12, figure size appropriate for two-column paper (3.5in or 7in width). Use colorblind-friendly palettes.

title: Generate supplementary analysis plots
description: Additional diagnostic and exploratory visualizations to support the paper's claims and provide deeper insight.

steps.step1: Per-program Shapley value heatmap: programs (rows) x passes (columns) heatmap showing how pass importance varies across programs. Cluster both axes. Save as results/figures/fig_supp_program_pass_heatmap.pdf.
steps.step2: Interaction consistency plot: scatter plot of interaction index on program A (x) vs program B (y) for all pass pairs, colored by program similarity. Save as results/figures/fig_supp_interaction_consistency.pdf.
steps.step3: Cost-effectiveness plot: code size reduction achieved vs number of compilation oracle calls, comparing Shapley-based approach (amortized) vs random search. Save as results/figures/fig_supp_cost_effectiveness.pdf.
